speaker: interviewer
text: With Stella Faitek on March the 6th, 1997. In-- in Birsk, did you hear anything about what was happening to the Jews? Did you get any news, any information?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have it in dreams. You wouldn't believe what dreams I had. We read in paper that it's very bad in Poland. And one time I had a deem that I was living by the Volga. I had a dream that the train is coming from Poland through the Volga. In the train will be my husband's mother and his sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I put on long shoes. And I'm running to Volga. And I'm running in Volga, and I saw the train. And I catched her hand-- his sister, her name was Manya and I catched her, and I said, where are you going? And said, don't ask me where I am going. Here is-- I know, she said, that you are sewing a black blouse. Here is a piece of green material. Make from the green material a collar to the black. It will be a little lively. And I said to her, tell me, where are you going? We are going to die. And she ran away.

speaker: interviewee
text: The train ran away, and I am walking with Volga, going back. And I was crying. I will never forget this dream. There, a few months later, we find out what is going on, how they killed people. Then I had other-- I had in dreams this what was going on in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the other dreams?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh Another dream with horses. They put on horses, and each horse has a line-- a wire. In every wire was a Jew put together. And then they give a-- a command for the horses to run. And all the people were killed by the horses. You cannot forget those dreams.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was terrible. I couldn't sleep at night. I was crying. My husband used to wake me up-- what are you-- what are you hollering? Lots of dreams. I saw everything in dreams. Did you ever meet any refugees who came to Birsk who told you what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the Russian?

speaker: interviewer
text: From what was happening to the Jews, maybe another Polish Jew, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't see, in Birsk, nobody. When we came back, there are papers. And we gave ads in papers-- we are looking for this person and this person. And I was looking in the papers, and I found in a paper a ad, Bella Szpinak with this-- with her mother from Warsaw is looking for friends and relatives-- it's his aunt and his cousin. And there is a address to go to the Jewish committee in Warsaw with the address.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was 1946?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1946. I came there, and I asked for them. And they said, we don't have on the list with that-- by this name. And I showed them the paper-- find out in Jewish committee. And they look. They cannot find it. And I sit there, and I holler, what is it? You have the paper. You have to look. Somewhere she is there. Finally, they found that she is in-- in a little city not far from Warsaw. I will recall it, the number. And I'm going in a train. This was after the pogrom in Kielce. And I'm going there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the train was sitting a little-- a lady. And I asked her, do you-- are you from the city? She said yes. I am looking in the city for my aunt and cousin, which supposed to be there, and I haven't got where to stay. And she said, you can stay with us. She took me to her house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were very nice to me, very good. It was on a Saturday. She said, today, the offices are closed. Tomorrow-- it was Sunday-- tomorrow we will find out. We went to [INAUDIBLE] to test the chief of the little city, Piastow-- the city was Piastow. We will ask him about the address. There is a address, but the address is in a church.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I asked the lady, do you know that some people were living here? She said, maybe in the time of war, maybe they were hiding here. The number is right. I have the number, but nobody's there with the name.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I decided to write-- to go back to Warsaw. And I wrote a ad, and I hanged it on the pharmacy and this stop-- bus stops, where is a bigger communication, Stella Faitek and Israel Faitek are looking for those and those, my address and telephone. I came back to Warsaw, and I told them there is nobody. They were hidden there in time of war. And I came back, and they told me another address. I came back again, I looked, nobody's there. Going back to Warsaw, I saw my girlfriend, which was hidden in ghetto. And I was  in Russia. And I'm very happy to see her. And I said to her, you know what, will you please be so kind, take this ad. Go to the paper. And give the ad  in paper, because I left my child home alone, and I have to rush home. She said OK. She took the ad. She went to the office there. She gave the ad to them. There was my cousin working there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh my--

speaker: interviewee
text: She took the ad. She recognized her, but she is playing-- working there as a Polish woman. She cannot tell her that she knows her. She takes the address. What life is going just like this-- if I would go, I would find her right away. But since she had the ad-- the ad in hand, with the telephone, I came back to Lodz. From Lodz, I came to Warsaw. I came there-- back to Lodz. There is a telephone from her. So I found her. Then I found a few more people through the paper. People were looking for each other. Then I was looking for my brother, and I found my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Joseph, the older one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because Sam was with you in Birsk the whole time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sam was with me, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did Joseph-- how did Joseph survive the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was working in Syktyvkar in the coal mine. He survived after the war. He came there, and he was in the German city, which was belonging later to Poland, Rychbach-- Reichenbach. And I came there, and I found him there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he was-- So he was in-- Joseph was also in Russia during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. He was in coal mine, working. I told you that he was with his cousin, walking to Russian border. And he survived through the concentration camp there. He was working all the time in coal mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: What experience with anti-Semitism in Poland did you have after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible. When the wagon crossed the border, there were lots of Polish people with stones and knocking on the wall. So many Jews still less-- left? So many Jews? You go back. It was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because this was a train of Jews who--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, coming back.

speaker: interviewer
text: --had been in Russia who wanted--

speaker: interviewee
text: Coming back to Poland. It was a terrible experience. And then we-- when we were in Poland, we had the Kielce pogrom and demonstrations on the street against the Jews. But we weren't long in Poland. We went out to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever afraid for your life in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: For sure. Sure. When we came back, we were very afraid. I was afraid to go out at night. But we weren't long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there-- was there any incident or anything-- anything in particular that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Kielce pogroms, just like now I see it. On the streets, very many-- yeah, I went to buy, uh, pillowcases. And the Polish woman was standing with the material-- it was like a bazaar here. And I asked her, with my husband I am staying there, how much it is. She said 2,000 zlotys. 2,000 zlotys was a lot of money. And I said, no, that's too much for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she looked at me, and my husband went away. She said, how come that you married a Jew? And I said, I'll tell you why. Uh. How come that you didn't-- what was it? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Somehow it came out that she asked me how come that you-- you are not married to a Jew. And I said I was married to a Jew. I married a Jew. I had my first-- oh, I remember now. I marry a Jew, because my first husband was a Polack, and he was beating me up. He was drinking. And now I have a Jewish husband. I know a life. He is very good to me, because I [? paid ?] [? her ?] [? back. ?] [LAUGHS] There was an incident on a train where you were in danger, and instant-- an instant where something almost fell on you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. This was a time when I was going to my brother, to Reichenbach. And we were sitting in train. My son was by the window.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, going from Lodz to Rychbach. In the other side from the window was sitting a woman. And I was sitting at the edge, and here a woman at the edge. And the woman-- no-- where we were sitting there, a woman came in.. And she pointed out who is Jewish. They took out from the train three Jews. They never came-- it was a stop. They never came back. The woman, with two policemen, came back. And she stopped by me, and she said, I think that you are Jewish, too. But here, I play with life. I said, I think that you are Jewish. My Polish language is beautiful. And she was from a little city, from-- far from Warsaw, from big city.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, what do we have to argue? She has a son here. This was a miracle. My son wasn't circumcised in Russia. They let the pants down. He wasn't circumcised. And she said, well, maybe she's not Jewish. A few minutes later, the lady across from my son by the window said-- asked me, will you please switch with me? I cannot sit by the window. It runs so fast I have a headache. I said OK. I switched with her. A few minutes later, a head from a sewing machine fells on her, and she was killed. Do you believe in destiny? This was my destiny. Twice in one-- in a few hours. Eventually, you went to Germany. And you first came to a-- a-- a-- a DP camp, a displaced persons camp in Ulm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ulm, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was '46. We were there until '48? '48, they send us to-- uh-- out from Ulm.

speaker: interviewer
text: To that was Fohrenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fohrenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in Ulm for two years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in Ulm. And from there, we went to Fohrenwald. In Fohrenwald, we went until-- Mark was born in 1950. In '51, we went out from Fohrenwald to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was life like in Ulm, in the camp in Ulm?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just the same, like it was in Ulm.

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- well--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Fohrenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no. How-- how-- oh, so--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you ask about Ulm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Ulm, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was-- before we came there, it was for military. And there on the street were apartments. We had our apartment by the big place. And everybody got their rations from the [NON-ENGLISH]. We lived very comfortable. We had everything. It was quiet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you hoping to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Ulm?

speaker: interviewer
text: From Ulm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to go to Canada, because my cousins were there. But we went through a commission. And my son had spina bifida, little bone on the back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was inter-- he was looked by a doctor, and he had a hand there. And when he slid the hand down, he felt the bone. He said to me, why didn't you tell me that he has tuberculosis, bone tuberculosis? I said, it's not bone tuberculosis. He has spina bifida. That's a lie. And he wrote that I lied. And they didn't accept me to go to Canada.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we wrote to my cousin. And my cousin said, I will try to get you there. In the meantime, there's a registration to go to America. So I registered to go to America, because they throw me out, and I don't know what will be farther. Then I got a letter. When I was registered to go to America, I got a note that I will go to a commission to go to America. I got a letter from my cousin, that she had a rich man, a millionaire, which guaranteed for us with $2 million to come to Canada. He will guarantee for everything, and you are coming to us. So in the meantime, we were called to the-- to go to America, so we went to America. And that's the way we are here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you never thought about going to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. To go to Israel, I think-- thought about going when I was in Germany. But at that time, there was no way to go, because Israel wasn't free. Israel was under the English.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean when you were in Poland, when you were in Poland?  Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in Poland, before the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: You thought to go to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: To the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. But after the war, you didn't think to go to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war, Israel wasn't Israel. Israel was Palestine, [INTERPOSING VOICES] so we couldn't think about it. And when Israel was free, when we could go, I felt very comfortable in America. But I went later on. I went to see Israel. I had lots of friends. I had relatives there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was life in Fohrenwald the-- the same kind of life as Ulm, was it more or less the same?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the same thing. The same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were supported by [INAUDIBLE], and by the [? Joint, ?] also, or mainly by [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. It was-- Later it wasn't [INAUDIBLE]. Later it was another name. But we got support from [? Joint, ?] too. But this was something extra. But everything, we got from [INAUDIBLE]. The food was very good. Everything was fine. And Mark was born in time when I was  in Fohrenwald. Mark was born in Munich, 1950.  [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any contact with Germans, with German-- the German population, while you were in Fohrenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother used to go out with a lady. Her husband was killed in the army. Was a very nice lady, a very fine lady. I used to correspond with her, about a year after I came back to Poland. Later, I didn't write to her anymore-- was a nice person.

speaker: interviewer
text: But once you were in Ulm and then in Fohrenwald, you never went back to Poland again? You just went to Poland from Russia for a while, and then after--

speaker: interviewee
text: From-- from Russia, we went back to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Back to Poland, and then from Poland to Ulm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then to Fohrenwald. But you never went back to Poland again.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back just for a visit, not to stay there. I was twice back to Poland, because I have two-- have a cousin there. Though in the second time-- the first time, I came to a conference, [INAUDIBLE] conference. All the children which are alive, the personnel which was working there, were meeting in Warsaw. There I was four weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was about eight, nine years ago. And then four years ago, I went back to Poland to see my cousins. And from that time, I never went back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once you came to this country-- excuse me, you came to Chicago, or eventually you came to Chicago, why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? I didn't come-- I wanted to go-- come to Chicago, but we were marked to go to-- not to Chicago, but to Atlanta, Georgia. My husband's a tailor. I had an uncle in Chicago, and I want to go to Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we came to New York, it was on a Friday. And it was before the holidays. And I decided to go to the [? Joint ?] to ask to send me to Chicago, because I have an uncle here, and it's closer to Toronto. My brother was in Toronto.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they gave me the permission to go to Toron-- to Chicago. But otherwise, I would be in Atlanta, because if you are chosen to go to America, they gave you a place where to go. You don't choose where do you want to go. But when I was here, I told them that I have a sister here. And I lied. It's not a-- not a sister. They came before us. It was a neighbor. And they checked that she is here, so they let me go to-- to Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was life like for you when you came to Chicago?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was strange. It was dirty. It was wind blow and all the papers and plastic on my face. And if you get-- got such a dirty-- beautiful city, dirty, flies everything. But I got used to it. [LAUGHS] The people were very friendly, very nice. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you live when you came-- when you first came here?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to hotel. We stayed in a hotel. And then my husband was looking for work. He start working. I had here a uncle, a aunt, two cousins. They came to see me in hotel. And, uh, we saw them from time to time. When I was settled in our apartment, I made a birthday party for the children. They were invited. We were a short time very close, but my uncle died. And then my aunt died, so it's a different life for me with my cousins. They are different than I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: In what way? They consider me lower from what they are. They called me [INAUDIBLE]. So-- and, uh, they think that they are higher. They asked me beginning, did you have a telephone? Did you have a washroom? Things which are unbelievable. I said, we had a telephone. We had washrooms. We had televisions. Televisions too? Yes, we had televisions.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then she-- I asked her to give me the telephone number to call her. And I write it. And she said to her husband, take a look, [? Sydney, ?] her beautiful handwriting. I said, what do you think, that we are coming from the woods? They-- they are different from us. Then, a time later, my uncle died. And he was in Drexel Home. Here is a home, Drexel. And she called me, if she can-- before he went to Drexel Home, she asked me if I want to take care of the uncle. And I said, how can I take care of your uncle-- of your father when I have two children, and I have two bedrooms? So they located him in Drexel Home.

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to come once a week there. He liked kreplach. He liked gefilte fish. I used to bring it to him. And one day, I came, and my uncle died the same day. And they called the daughter. She came, and from that time on, we are not close anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your second child was also a hemophiliac?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had a very difficult time.

speaker: interviewee
text: You said it. And a sick husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: With ulcer.

speaker: interviewee
text: With ulcer.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he worked as a furrier.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He worked in-- here in Chicago as a furrier. And we made a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- You worked also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. My children were at school, special school. They were very good students until they grew up. Leon worked. And Mark was working, little by little, so we managed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think your experiences with Dr. Korczak influenced the way that you raised your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it's harder to raise your own children than childrens-- strange children. It's very hard, especially in the case of hemophilia. You have to be very careful. You have to be strict with them-- don't do this, don't do this, don't do this. I knew, and I felt that sometimes, they hate me. But I hadn't got a choice. One time, Leon said to me, ma, I know that you are right, but I cannot take all the time no, no, no. I will not listen to you. Even you are right, I will not listen. Mark was at that time four years old, and he want to play baseball. He want to do-- to go on a bicycle. I said, you cannot go play ball, because you have to run, and you might fell-- fell. You can't go on a bicycle, because you have to drive with your legs, but you have very often swollen legs. But I took them to play tennis, ping pong. I bought that table. We played ping pong, tennis. But they want something else. They had something in sport in themself from me. They want to do things. So I knew, and I felt that they hate me sometimes. It's hard to raise children, your own. But in a case like this, it's very, very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your husband also was sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ulcers. And he played the role that he is always sick, never helped me. But he was a sick person.

speaker: interviewer
text: How, as far as your adjusting to-- to life in this country, was it? Of course, it was made more difficult by the-- your family situation. But do you feel that after-- after a time, you did adjust to living here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I adjust to everything. Everything is easier for me. I know that Leon has a marvelous wife. Mark has a wife. So my responsibilities not so much. I enjoy it now, life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you feel that now, in your-- you're-- as you're older, that you enjoy life more than--

speaker: interviewee
text: I enjoy life. And I try to give something from my life. I volunteer 12 years in a old home, Northwest Home. I work there three times a week, three hours every time. And I am satisfied that I can help. This gives me a satisfaction.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, it's by volunteering, you get a lot of satis-- you can get a lot of satisfaction.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right. I know that I help somebody. OK, I know that we're--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So we'll--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
